Carboxylic Acids
Carboxylic acids contain the functional group –C ( =O )OH
Carboxylic acids are ubiquitious in biochemistry and other applications of organic chemistry
The bound –OH group makes them significantly different from aldehydes and ketones

Section 19.1
Carboxylic Acid Nomenclature

Naming Carboxylic Acids
Many carboxylic acids are known by their common names
Formic acid
Acetic acid
Benzoic acid
Butyric acid
Valeric acid
In systematic names, –e in the parent alkane name is replaced with –oic acid
Propanoic acid
To name diacids, replace –e in the parent alkane name with –edioic acid
Propanedioic acid (malonic acid )
Naming Carboxylic Acids-1

Section 19.2
Structure and Bonding

Structure of Carboxylic Acids
Carboxylic acids have sp 2 hybridization at the carbonyl carbon, one C=O (σ + π) and one C–OH
Carboxylic acids contain a delocalized π system
The carbonyl carbon receives electron density from – O H, and is less electrophilic than carbonyl C in ketones and aldehydes
The best electrophile in RCO 2 H is the acidic hydrogen!
The best nucleophile is the carbonyl oxygen ( not the –OH oxygen )

Section 19.3
Physical Properties

Physical Properties of RCO 2 H
The melting and boiling points of RCO 2 H are considerably higher than those of related compounds
RCO 2 H can hydrogen twice in an intramolecular fashion
Dimeric structures lead to high melting and boiling points

Section 19.4
Acidity of Carboxylic Acids

Acidity of Carboxylic Acids
Carboxylic acids are moderately acidic at CO 2 H . p K a ’s range from 2 – 6
Note that carboxylic acids are still weak acids!
The conjugate base of a carboxylic acid ( carboxylate ) is stable relative to an alkoxide

Acidity of Carboxylic Acids-1
Carboxylic acids are moderately acidic at CO 2 H . p K a ’s range from 2 – 6
Note that carboxylic acids are still weak acids!
The conjugate base of a carboxylic acid ( carboxylate ) is stable relative to an alkoxide
The carbonyl group is inductively electron withdrawing

Acidity of Carboxylic Acids-2
Carboxylic acids are moderately acidic at CO 2 H . p K a ’s range from 2 – 6
Note that carboxylic acids are still weak acids!
The conjugate base of a carboxylic acid ( carboxylate ) is stable relative to an alkoxide
Resonance illustrates delocalization of negative charge in carboxylate

Carboxylic Acids in Solution
In a buffered solution of pH 7, a carboxylic acid with p K a 5 is mostly in the carboxylate form !
From the given pH and p K a , we can calculate the concentrations of acid and conjugate base
Under biochemical conditions (pH 7), acids are usually referred to as carboxylates ( pyruvate , lactate )

Section 19.5
SubstiTuents and Acids Strength

Substituent Effects: Induction
Alkyl substituents have a minimal effect on acidity; all alkyl acids have p K a around 5
Electronegative substituents increase acidity via inductive effects
The usual distance dependence of induction is observed
Survey of RCO 2 H Acidity

Section 19.6
Ionization of Substituted Benzoic Acids

Acidity of Benzoic Acids
Benzoic acid is slightly more acidic than acetic acid due to electronegativity of the sp 2 hybridized benzene carbon
Substituents that are withdrawing or donating by resonance exert strong effects on acidity when positioned ortho or para

Acidity of Benzoic Acids-1
Consider resonance forms of the conjugate base and inductive effects .
